source.title: Chunking during human visuomotor sequence learning

confidence_rationale: Direct human-behavioural evidence for spontaneous chunking with quantified chunk-size constraints (typically 2-4 items, sometimes 5). This is the load-bearing R5 source for parameterising the outcome-consistency threshold and the chunk-size budget. The reference-time-gap operationalisation (chunks identified by inter-element-time clustering) is a clean behavioural signature ARC-071 implementations can mirror. Source is the original Sakai paper, though the briefing mistakenly placed it in Curr Op Neurobiol (it is in Exp Brain Res). Mapping fidelity 0.78 -- the chunk-size-budget evidence translates directly; the reference task is finger-sequence learning so the motor-chunk grain is fine-grained.
failure_signatures: Chunks are typically 2-4 items, sometimes 5 -- ARC-071 should default to small-grain chunks; chunks-of-15 are biologically unusual and likely indicate over-aggressive accumulator parameters.

Chunks are identified by inter-element TIME GAPS -- ARC-071's chunk-execution signature should emit a faster within-chunk timing than between-chunk timing; this is a direct behavioural test of whether chunks formed.
Subjects spontaneously chunk during sequence learning without instruction -- ARC-071 implementation should fire automatically, not require explicit goal-driven chunking instruction.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Motor sequence learning is a process whereby a series of elementary movements is re-coded into an efficient representation for the entire sequence. Human subjects learn a visuomotor sequence by spontaneously chunking the elementary movements; each chunk acts as a single memory unit. Subjects perform sequences as several clusters of sets separated by long time gaps; clusters typically contain 2-4 items, sometimes 5. As learning progresses, clusters become clearer and more consistent; each cluster is processed as a single memory unit, a chunk, and is necessary for efficient sequence processing.
mapping.ree_translation: ARC-071's chunk-size budget is small (2-5 elements) per the canonical biological pattern. The behavioural signature of chunk formation is inter-element timing -- shorter within-chunk, longer between-chunk gaps. ARC-071 implementations should produce this signature when chunks form. The spontaneous-chunking-without-instruction finding supports ARC-071 firing automatically (not requiring explicit goal-driven chunking instruction). For R5 outcome-consistency threshold: the cluster-becomes-clearer-with-learning trajectory implies a gradual variance threshold rather than a step function.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Sakai et al. study is on finger-sequence learning -- a fine-grained motor task. The chunk-size budget (2-5) may not transfer cleanly to coarser action primitives (e.g. ARC-007 hippocampal proposals at single-action grain over many seconds). REE's 'primitives' are coarser than Sakai's 'elements'; the chunk-size budget should be calibrated separately at the REE-primitive grain. Also, the paper is in Exp Brain Res, not Curr Op Neurobiol as the briefing pre-registered.
mapping.source_context: Healthy human subjects performing 2x10 visuomotor sequence task with extensive practice; chunk identification via inter-element time-gap analysis.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality 0.75 (Exp Brain Res, well-cited but not top-tier). Mapping fidelity 0.78 (direct on chunk-size budget; transfer to coarser-grain REE primitives is the open question). Transfer risk 0.30 -- the task-grain mismatch is the main risk; the behavioural signature should generalise but the chunk-size constants may not.
